spans.text: Letterhead Factum
118 Ochterloney Street
Dartmouth, Nova Scotia
B2Y 1C7
THE HONOURABLE JUSTICE JEFFREY R. HU
Supreme Court of Nova Scotia
The Law Courts Building
1815 Upper Water St.
Halifax, NS  B3J 1S7
MY LORD:
Re: Leslie J. Conrad, Edwin Conrad, Dan Merzetti, and Sherri Merzetti v. Arichat Metal Fabrication Ltd., and Gregory Boucher, and John Boudreau; Hfx No. 496393
INTRODUCTION

1. This application in chambers is scheduled before Your Lordship on July 26, 2023 at 2:00 p.m., on a motion for summary judgment on the evidence dismissing the proceeding pursuant to the Limitation of Actions Act.1 Micaela Sheppard and I represent the respondents, Arichat Metal Fabrication Ltd. (“Arichat Metal”) and John Boudreau, the moving parties of the motion (together, “AMF Respondents”). The applicants are represented by Michelle Kelly and Lisa Delaney. The co-respondent, Gregory Boucher, and the third party, 1766134 Nova Scotia Limited (“176 NSL”), are represented by Adam Harris.

2. The AMF Respondents had filed a motion for summary judgment on the evidence on April 1, 2021 (amended April 8, 2021; further amended May 24, 2023). In support, the AMF Respondents filed a brief on April 8, 2021 and the applicants filed a brief on May 20, 2021. Since that time, however, the parties have dealt with a motion for production and discovery, which resulted in the production of crucial new evidence to the AMF Respondents which we now rely on in support of the present motion.
1 Limitation of Actions Act, SNS 2014, c 35, “LAA”.

3. Also, since our first brief filed on April 8, 2021, the Supreme Court of Canada has issued new guidance with respect to the law upon which this motion is brought, in particular the “discoverability principle”, in Grant Thornton LLP v New Brunswick, 2021 SCC 31 (“Grant”).

4. As well, it became clear after the 2021 filings that we as counsel for the AMF Respondents did not adequately appreciate the legal issue and applicable law in our prior submissions, and that revised briefing was necessary for the court to be fully apprised of the analysis which it is tasked to undertake.

5. Therefore, counsel has agreed that supplemental briefing is appropriate in this motion. This brief is intended to supplant fully our brief filed April 8, 2021, and the court need not refer to that brief. If any party wishes to file supplemental briefing in response to this brief, then our clients consent to such filing.

6. The parties also agreed to supplemental affidavits in this motion, and we are filing an affidavit attaching an email between the applicants’ former legal counsel and their insurance adjuster as supplemental evidence on this motion.
PROCEDURAL BACKGROUND
Letterhead Factum
118 Ochterloney Street
Dartmouth, Nova Scotia
B2Y 1C7
THE HONOURABLE JUSTICE JEFFREY R. HU
Supreme Court of Nova Scotia
The Law Courts Building
1815 Upper Water St.
Halifax, NS  B3J 1S7
MY LORD:
Re: Leslie J. Conrad, Edwin Conrad, Dan Merzetti, and Sherri Merzetti v. Arichat Metal Fabrication Ltd., and Gregory Boucher, and John Boudreau; Hfx No. 496393
INTRODUCTION

1. This application in chambers is scheduled before Your Lordship on July 26, 2023 at 2:00 p.m., on a motion for summary judgment on the evidence dismissing the proceeding pursuant to the Limitation of Actions Act.1 Micaela Sheppard and I represent the respondents, Arichat Metal Fabrication Ltd. (“Arichat Metal”) and John Boudreau, the moving parties of the motion (together, “AMF Respondents”). The applicants are represented by Michelle Kelly and Lisa Delaney. The co-respondent, Gregory Boucher, and the third party, 1766134 Nova Scotia Limited (“176 NSL”), are represented by Adam Harris.

2. The AMF Respondents had filed a motion for summary judgment on the evidence on April 1, 2021 (amended April 8, 2021; further amended May 24, 2023). In support, the AMF Respondents filed a brief on April 8, 2021 and the applicants filed a brief on May 20, 2021. Since that time, however, the parties have dealt with a motion for production and discovery, which resulted in the production of crucial new evidence to the AMF Respondents which we now rely on in support of the present motion.
1 Limitation of Actions Act, SNS 2014, c 35, “LAA”.

3. Also, since our first brief filed on April 8, 2021, the Supreme Court of Canada has issued new guidance with respect to the law upon which this motion is brought, in particular the “discoverability principle”, in Grant Thornton LLP v New Brunswick, 2021 SCC 31 (“Grant”).

4. As well, it became clear after the 2021 filings that we as counsel for the AMF Respondents did not adequately appreciate the legal issue and applicable law in our prior submissions, and that revised briefing was necessary for the court to be fully apprised of the analysis which it is tasked to undertake.

5. Therefore, counsel has agreed that supplemental briefing is appropriate in this motion. This brief is intended to supplant fully our brief filed April 8, 2021, and the court need not refer to that brief. If any party wishes to file supplemental briefing in response to this brief, then our clients consent to such filing.

6. The parties also agreed to supplemental affidavits in this motion, and we are filing an affidavit attaching an email between the applicants’ former legal counsel and their insurance adjuster as supplemental evidence on this motion.
PROCEDURAL BACKGROUND

7. The applicants in this proceeding previously filed a notice of action on June 29, 2010 against 176 NSL, then-named “AFL Tank Manufacturing Limited”, in Hfx No. 331744 (“2010 Action”). In the 2010 Action, the applicants claimed losses in negligence arising from the leakage of oil from oil tanks which had been manufactured by 176 NSL. That proceeding did not name the respondents to this proceeding, Arichat Metal, Mr. Boudreau, and Mr. Boucher, nor did it name 3236552 Nova Scotia Limited (“323 NSL”).

8. 176 NSL filed a notice of withdrawal of its defence and consent to judgment on February 13, 2018. On or around January 24, 2019, Justice Timothy Gabriel assessed the applicants’ damages and awarded the applicants judgment against the defendant AFL Tank Manufacturing Ltd, at that time known as 176 NSL, in the 2010 Proceeding in the amount of $754,262.63. However, the applicants did not collect judgment from 176 NSL.

9. Then, on February 7th, 2020, the applicants filed the present application in court against Arichat Metal, Mr. Boudreau, and Mr. Boucher, alleging that the respondents are liable on a joint and several basis for the judgment issued in the 2010 Proceeding against 176 NSL. The applicants further allege that an asset purchase-and-sale transaction between 176 NSL and Arichat Metal was a sham.

10. In 2021, the respondents filed a notice of motion for summary judgment on the evidence, alleging that the applicants’ claim against the respondents is out of time pursuant to the Limitation of Action Act. The applicants filed the affidavit of Scott Johnson, sworn on May 20, 2021 (“Johnson Affidavit”), in support of their contest of the respondents’ motion for summary judgment. Mr. Johnson, a national property claim consultant with Allstate Insurance Company of Canada (“Allstate”), is the insurance adjuster for the applicants. Allstate became subrogated to the rights of the applicants in the 2010 Proceeding.2

11. Upon receiving the Johnson Affidavit, the AMF Respondents determined that his cross-examination at the motion for summary judgment would be necessary. This was not possible because the hearing had been scheduled to be heard via videoconference due to the pandemic, and because of the court’s protocols at the time. As a result, the summary judgment motion was adjourned without date, although an adjournment would have been needed anyway due to the need for production which arose from the Johnson Affidavit.

12. The respondents filed a motion for production, and also for discovery of legal counsel witnesses, on November 16, 2021, arguing that paragraph 16 of the Johnson Affidavit constituted waiver of solicitor/client privilege over the conversation between Mr. Johnson and the former counsel for the applicants, Shelley Wood. In particular, the respondents sought production of an email sent from Ms. Wood to Mr. Johnson on December 20, 2016 (“2016 Email”), in which the new corporate entity, Arichat Metal, was discussed.

13. Justice Glen G. McDougall heard the motion for production and discovery on March 15, 2022. By written decision on October 14, 2022, McDougall J. allowed the production of the 2016 Email, with redactions, to the respondents and third party, agreeing that privilege had been waived over that communication. Justice McDougall did not grant the motion for discovery of legal counsel witnesses.
2 Johnson Affidavit, paras 1 and 8.

14. The AMF Respondents amended their motion for summary judgment on the evidence against the applicants on May 26, 2023, and now file the affidavit of Brittany Durnford, sworn July 10, 2023, which attaches the 2016 Email, in support.
FACTS

15. As early as 2006, Mr. Boucher and Mr. Boudreau began discussing and negotiating the sale of 176 NSL’s assets. This transaction transpired over the next several years, concluding in or around November 27, 2015, when 176 NSL received payment of $386,213.60.3 The applicants in this proceeding started an action against 176 NSL, then AFL Tank Manufacturing Limited, on June 29, 2010.4 On December 20, 2010, Mr. Boudreau incorporated a new entity, then known as 3250450 Nova Scotia Limited (“325 NSL”), and eventually carrying on business as Arichat Metal.5 On December 31, 2010, 176 NSL changed its name from “AFL Tank Manufacturing Limited” to its present name of 1766134 Nova Scotia Limited.6 This meant that the party defending the legal action was now 1766134 Nova Scotia Limited, although the court record was never updated to reflect this. On the same date, 325 NSL changed its name to AFL Tank Manufacturing Limited.7 These changes resulted from an asset purchase-and-sale between the two companies, as well as another company of Mr. Boudreau’s, 323 NSL.

16. The respondents say that these actions, including assigning the business name of AFL Tank Manufacturing to 325 NSL, were all legitimately performed pursuant to an ordinary asset purchase-and-sale transaction for fair market value, exchanging the assets of 176 NSL for cash and leaving value in 176 NSL for any future judgment against it in lieu of the transferred assets. The transaction was not surreptitious. The parties made all necessary public registrations with the Registry of Joint Stock Companies.
3 Johnson Affidavit, Exhibit “I”.
4 Johnson Affidavit, Exhibit “A”.
5 Affidavit of Lindsay Corey, sworn April 1, 2021, Exhibit “A”, [“Corey Affidavit”] at para 19 of the Affidavit of Jeffrey Waugh, filed April 13, 2018 [“Waugh Affidavit”]
6 Corey Affidavit, Exhibit “A”, at para 10 of the Affidavit of Jeffrey Waugh, filed April 13, 2018 [“Waugh Affidavit”].
7 Corey Affidavit, Exhibit “A”, at para 12 of Waugh Affidavit.

17. Meanwhile, the 2010 Action against 176 NSL continued through disclosure and discovery examinations. The applicants discovered Mr. Boucher on April 9, 2013. Mr. Boucher deposed that at that time, he was no longer the president of “AFL”, and that Mr. Boudreau, his business partner, was.10

18. On October 22, 2015, the applicants received correspondence from Ralph Ripley, counsel for 325 NSL, which was carrying on business as AFL Tank Manufacturing at the time.11 Mr. Ripley’s letter discussed the incorporation of 325 NSL in 2010 and the fact that 176 NSL no longer bore the name “AFL Tank Manufacturing Limited” but that 325 NSL then did. This correspondence attached printouts of the corporate profiles for 325 NSL and 176 NSL. At the time, both Mr. Boudreau and Mr. Boucher were directors and officers of 325 NSL.

19. The applicants screen-captured the corporate profile of Arichat Metal on December 16, 2016 from the Registry of Joint Stock Companies website.12 This profile indicated that the company formerly known as 3250450 Nova Scotia Limited changed its name a second time from AFL Tank Manufacturing Limited to Arichat Metal Fabrication Limited. The 2016 corporate profile indicated that Mr. Boudreau was its sole director and officer, having removed Mr. Boucher on or around December 2, 2015.

20. On December 20, 2016, Ms. Wood emailed Scott Johnson, the insurance adjuster for the applicants.13 In that email, Ms. Wood informed Mr. Johnson that the applicants had perused the Registry of Joint Stock Companies and that they were “searching the public registries for any information” regarding AFL/176 NSL’s assets and liabilities. She further suggested that Mr. Boucher and AFL/176 NSL was “judgment proof”.

21. On April 18, 2018, Ms. Wood and Mr. Johnson spoke on the phone whereby Ms. Wood advised Mr. Johnson that Arichat Metal had, in fact, purchased the assets of 176 NSL.14 She further advised him that the company formerly known as AFL, and now called 176 NSL, may not have any assets on which to recover, while Arichat Metal was a going concern.
10 Corey Affidavit, Exhibit “A”, at para 22 of Waugh Affidavit.
11 Corey Affidavit, Exhibit “C”.
12 Corey Affidavit, Exhibit “B”.
13 Affidavit of Brittany Durnford, sworn July 10, 2023, Exhibit “A”.
14 Johnson Affidavit, para 16.

22. At no point in the 2010 Action did the applicants amend the style of cause to name 323 NSL, 325 NSL, John Boudreau, or Arichat Metal as parties. The applicants did not subpoena nor otherwise seek disclosure from any of these entities or individuals. The applicants did not recall Mr. Boucher for discovery or otherwise require him to disclose evidence pertaining to what if any disposition 176 NSL made of the proceeds of sale of its assets, or to whom.

23. In support of the 2010 Action, the applicants had filed the affidavit of Jeffrey Waugh, then-articled clerk for Stewart McKelvey, which affidavit was sworn on April 13, 2018. The Waugh Affidavit and some of its exhibits were incorporated into the present proceeding by virtue of the affidavit of Lindsay Corey, sworn on April 1, 2021; the entire Waugh Affidavit was incorporated into this proceeding via the Johnson Affidavit, sworn April 13, 2021.
ISSUES
24. Should the court dismiss the application summarily on the grounds that the applicants’ claim is barred by the Limitation of Actions Act?
LAW & ARGUMENT
25. Summary judgment on the evidence is governed by Rule 13 of the Civil Procedure Rules. In particular, R. 13.04 says, as follows:
13.04 (1) A judge who is satisfied on both of the following must grant summary judgment on a claim or a defence in an action:
(a) there is no genuine issue of material fact, whether on its own or mixed with a question of law, for trial of the claim or defence;

(b) the claim or defence does not require determination of a question of law, whether on its own or mixed with a question of fact, or the claim or defence requires determination only of a question of law and the judge exercises the discretion provided in this Rule 13.04 to determine the question.

(2) When the absence of a genuine issue of material fact for trial and the absence of a question of law requiring determination are established, summary judgment must be granted without distinction between a claim and a defence and without further inquiry into chances of success.
(3) The judge may grant judgment, dismiss the proceeding, allow a claim, dismiss a claim, or dismiss a defence.

(4) On a motion for summary judgment on evidence, the pleadings serve only to indicate the issues, and the subjects of a genuine issue of material fact and a question of law depend on the evidence presented.

(5) A party who wishes to contest the motion must provide evidence in favour of the party’s claim or defence by affidavit filed by the contesting party, affidavit filed by another party, cross-examination, or other means permitted by a judge.
(6) A judge who hears a motion for summary judgment on evidence has discretion to do either of the following:
(a) determine a question of law, if there is no genuine issue of material fact for trial;
(b) adjourn the hearing of the motion for any just purpose including to permit necessary disclosure, production, discovery, presentation of expert evidence, or collection of other evidence.

26. This Rule has been expanded upon by the Nova Scotia Court of Appeal in its leading decision of Shannex Inc v Dora Construction Ltd.15 Justice Fichaud, writing for the court, provided the following guidance regarding summary judgment motions under Rule 13.04:
34 I interpret the amended Rule 13.04 to pose five sequential questions:
• First Question: Does the challenged pleading disclose a "genuine issue of material fact", either pure or mixed with a question of law? [Rules 13.04(1), (2) and (4)]
15 Shannex Inc v Dora Construction Ltd, 2016 NSCA 89 [“Shannex”].

If Yes, it should not be determined by summary judgment. It should either be considered for conversion to an application under Rules 13.08(1)(b) and 6 as discussed below [paras. 37-42], or go to trial.
The analysis of this question follows Burton’s first step.

A “material fact” is one that would affect the result. A dispute about an incidental fact - i.e. one that would not affect the outcome - will not derail a summary judgment motion: 2420188 Nova Scotia Ltd. v. Hiltz, 2011 NSCA 74 (N.S. C.A.), para. 27, adopted by Burton, para. 41, and see also para. 87 (#8).

The moving party has the onus to show by evidence there is no genuine issue of material fact. But the judge’s assessment is based on all the evidence from any source. If the pleadings dispute the material facts, and the evidence on the motion fails to negate the existence of a genuine issue of material fact, then the onus bites and the judge answers the first question Yes. [Rules 13.04(4) and (5)]

Burton, paras. 85-86, said that, if the responding party reasonably requires time to marshal his evidence, the judge should adjourn the motion for summary judgment. Summary judgment isn’t an ambush. Neither is the adjournment permission to procrastinate. The amended Rule 13.04(6)(b) allows the judge to balance these factors.
• Second Question: If the answer to #1 is No, then: Does the challenged pleading require the determination of a question of law, either pure, or mixed with a question of fact?

If the answers to #1 and #2 are both No, summary judgment “must” issue: Rules 13.04(1) and (2). This would be a nuisance claim with no genuine issue of any kind — whether material fact, law, or mixed fact and law.

• Third Question: If the answers to #1 and #2 are No and Yes respectively, leaving only an issue of law, then the judge “may” grant or deny summary judgment: Rule 13.04(3). Governing that discretion is the principle in Burton’s second test: “Does the challenged pleading have a real chance of success?”

Nothing in the amended Rule 13.04 changes Burton’s test. It is difficult to envisage any other principled standard for a summary judgment. To dismiss summarily, without a full merits analysis, a claim or defence that has a real chance of success at a later trial or application hearing, would be a patently unjust exercise of discretion.
It is for the responding party to show a real chance of success. If the answer is No, then summary judgment issues to dismiss the ill-fated pleading.

• Fourth Question: If the answer to #3 is Yes, leaving only an issue of law with a real chance of success, then, under Rule 13.04(6)(a): Should the judge exercise the “discretion” to finally determine the issue of law?

If the judge does not exercise this discretion, then: (1) the judge dismisses the motion for summary judgment, and (2) the matter with a “real chance of success” goes onward either to a converted application under Rules 13.08(1)(b) and 6, as discussed below [paras. 37-42], or to trial. If the judge exercises the discretion, he or she determines the full merits of the legal issue once and for all. Then the judge’s conclusion generates issue estoppel, subject to any appeal.

This is not the case to catalogue the principles that will govern the judge’s discretion under Rule 13.04(6)(a). Those principles will develop over time. Proportionality criteria, such as those discussed in Hryniak v. Mauldin, [2014] 1 S.C.R. 87 (S.C.C.), will play a role.

A party who wishes the judge to exercise discretion under Rule 13.04(6)(a) should state that request, with notice to the other party. The judge who, on his or her own motion, intends to exercise the discretion under Rule 13.04(6)(a) should notify the parties that the point is under consideration. Then, after the hearing, the judge’s decision should state whether and why the discretion was exercised. The reasons for this process are obvious: (1) fairness requires that both parties know the ground rules and whether the ruling will generate issue estoppel; (2) the judge’s standard differs between summary mode (“real chance of success”) and full-merits mode; (3) the judge’s choice may affect the standard of review on appeal.
[emphasis original]

27. The test for summary judgment on the evidence has been applied in situations where a limitation period to file a claim has expired.16 In this case, we submit that the two-year limitation period in the Limitation of Actions Act applies. Section 8 of the LAA is as follows:
16 See: Cameron v Nova Scotia Association of Health Organizations Long Term Disability Plan, 2019 NSCA 30.
8 (1) Unless otherwise provided in this Act, a claim may not be brought after the earlier of
(a) two years from the day on which the claim is discovered; and
(b) fifteen years from the day on which the act or omission on which the claim is based occurred.
(2) A claim is discovered on the day on which the claimant first knew or ought reasonably to have known
(a) that the injury, loss or damage had occurred;
(b) that the injury, loss or damage was caused by or contributed to by an act or omission;
(c) that the act or omission was that of the defendant; and
(d) that the injury, loss or damage is sufficiently serious to warrant a proceeding.
(3) For the purpose of clause (1)(b), the day an act or omission on which a claim is based occurred is
(a) in the case of a continuous act or omission, the day on which the act or omission ceases; and
(b) in the case of a series of acts or omissions concerning the same obligation, the day on which the last act or omission in the series occurs.

28. Under the first prong of the Shannex test, above, the moving party must show that there is no genuine material fact in dispute. In a case where the moving party argues the limitation period expired, they must show that the claimant discovered or ought to have discovered their loss or injury was the result of an act or omission caused by the defendant. The claimant’s knowledge of the loss may be actual or constructive, as discussed by the Supreme Court of Canada in Grant Thornton LLP v New Brunswick,17 as follows:

In my view, a claim is discovered when the plaintiff has knowledge, actual or constructive, of the material facts upon which a plausible inference of liability on the defendant’s part can be drawn. It follows from this standard that a plaintiff does not need knowledge of all the constituent elements of a claim to discover that claim.
17 Grant, para 3.
